lens.included_activities: Commercial game preserves and hunting preserves without accommodation, including private operators selling access to managed land, seasonal memberships, repeat hunt access, or preserve-use packages to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Commercial hunters and trappers whose principal revenue is pelts, meat, or other harvested products; hunting guides and outfitters; wildlife and pest-control services; hunting camps with accommodation; and nature preserves.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Individuals, member groups, and occasional organizational customers pay fixed access fees, memberships, and hunt or preserve-use package prices; revenue depends on managed wildlife populations, land access, safety, and the quality of the preserve experience.
lens.deviation_from_default: The six-digit industry combines product-harvesting hunters and trappers with commercial game and hunting preserves, which have different work and revenue models. The lens is narrowed to non-accommodation preserve operators solely to produce a coherent service-business analysis; Census cross-references keep guides, pest control, lodging, and nature preserves outside the code.

a.bridge: USGS evidence that AI can process wildlife imagery and support population monitoring is mapped to preserve inventory, habitat, and patrol-planning work, while BLS descriptions of hunters' physical field tasks bound the non-automatable share. Preserve operators mix those workflows with booking, marketing, scheduling, waivers, and customer administration. The proxy may overstate exposure where small preserves have few cameras or little digitized administration and understate it where monitoring and bookings are already digital.
a.rationale: AI can assist camera-trap review, species and abundance monitoring, inquiry handling, marketing content, booking, scheduling, membership administration, pricing support, waivers, and compliance documentation. Land and habitat maintenance, animal handling, customer safety and orientation, retrieval, and other field execution remain physical.
a.change_evidence: Time-and-task studies from commercial hunting preserves, especially the shares of labor spent on monitoring, administration, guest service, and field maintenance, would materially tighten the range.
caveats: The BLS occupation covers fishing and commercial hunting work rather than the narrowed preserve-operator lens.
No direct preserve-level task census or AI adoption study was found.

rho.bridge: USGS camera-trap systems with greater than 80% classification accuracy, F1 scores of at least 0.9, and documented analyst-hour savings are used as a technical-feasibility proxy for preserve monitoring. Realized substitution is discounted for rural connectivity, small-firm implementation capacity, false positives, safety and liability review, regulation, and the need to connect model outputs to field action. The proxy may overstate realization because a research workflow has better data and validation than a small preserve, but may understate it for standardized administrative work.
rho.rationale: A meaningful portion of exposed monitoring and document workflows can be deployed with current tools, but preserve-specific data quality, seasonal conditions, fragmented systems, human review, and field execution keep realized replacement below exposed work.
rho.change_evidence: Commercial deployments reporting preserve-level adoption, error rates, implementation costs, staff hours saved, and sustained use across seasons would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Research-grade wildlife monitoring does not directly establish economical deployment in small commercial preserves.
Safety-critical and regulatory decisions retain human accountability.

e.rationale: Within this heterogeneous code, some hypothetical lower-middle-market firms would be coherent recurring-access preserve businesses, while others would primarily harvest and sell animal products. The narrowed service lens is therefore unlikely to represent all firms in the code, but could represent a substantial portion of firms large enough to reach the target size band.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue-model classification, establishment descriptions, and size-band receipts split between preserves and commercial hunters or trappers would determine the eligible share.
caveats: The frozen dataset reports no lower-middle-market firms in this industry, so the parameter describes a hypothetical composition rather than observed eligible firms.
The Census industry definition does not publish a preserve-versus-harvesting firm split.

s5.rationale: Private land, operating permissions, customer relationships, and a maintained wildlife program can support a transfer, but many preserve businesses are closely tied to an owner, family property, or real estate and may transfer as assets, remain within families, close, or fall outside an operating-company sale process.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of preserve transactions, closures, family transfers, asset sales, and going-concern acquisitions would replace this judgmental range.
caveats: No preserve-specific transaction-rate evidence was found.
The frozen dataset reports no lower-middle-market firms, making the practical transaction count zero under the assigned inputs.

q.rationale: Preserve access, memberships, and hunt packages are typically sold at posted or negotiated package prices rather than cost-plus rates, allowing some implemented labor savings to be retained until renewal or competitive repricing. Capture is incomplete because land, habitat, wildlife, insurance, and guest experience remain central to value, and competition can return savings through improved service or lower package prices.
q.change_evidence: Preserve contribution-margin histories before and after automation, contract and membership renewal behavior, and local price comparisons would reveal how much cost reduction remains with the operator.
caveats: National hunting expenditure categories do not measure preserve pricing or cost pass-through directly.
Customer willingness to pay may depend more on access and wildlife quality than on administrative efficiency.

d5.bridge: National participation and expenditure in hunting are used as the demand proxy for paid preserve access. The base is held roughly flat over five years because 2022 showed 14.4 million hunters and $45.2 billion of hunting expenditures, while FWS also reports indications of a longer-run participation decline. Preserve demand could outperform total hunting where private access is scarce, or underperform because of demographics, regulation, and discretionary spending. Survey-method changes prevent treating the 2016-to-2022 difference as a clean trend.
d5.rationale: Hunting remains a large paid activity, supporting durable preserve demand, but hunter recruitment and retention concerns limit confidence in growth. Private access scarcity and managed experiences offer upside, while participation, regulation, weather, wildlife health, and discretionary-income sensitivity create downside.
d5.change_evidence: A consistent-method time series for preserve visits, memberships, package prices, hunter participation, and private-land access would materially change the five-year demand factor.
caveats: National hunting expenditure includes equipment, travel, licenses, and land-related spending beyond commercial preserves.
The 2022 national survey changed collection methodology, reducing comparability with prior surveys.

o.rationale: Even with automated booking, self-check-in, and AI-assisted wildlife monitoring, a commercial preserve still needs an accountable operator for land, habitat and wildlife stewardship, access, customer safety, regulation, liability, and physical upkeep. Technology can make the role leaner but is unlikely to remove the operator from a surviving preserve business within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of fully remote or operatorless preserves, regulatory acceptance of autonomous safety and wildlife decisions, and sustained customer adoption would lower this range.
caveats: The estimate concerns survival of an accountable operating role, not preservation of every current job or task.
Very small properties may be managed as passive land access rather than staffed operating businesses.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent service business inside this heterogeneous industry is the commercial game or hunting preserve. AI can reduce monitoring and administrative work, but the frozen firm count is zero and the physical, regulated, land-based operating role remains.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest applications are automated camera-trap review and wildlife monitoring, plus booking, inquiries, marketing, scheduling, pricing support, memberships, waivers, and compliance documents. Habitat maintenance, animal handling, guest safety, and other field work remain human and physical.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed access, membership, and package pricing permits some cost savings to remain with the preserve, although competition and customer expectations can convert savings into better service, and land and wildlife costs continue to dominate economics.
narrative.firm_availability: The code mixes preserves with commercial hunting and trapping for products, and published data do not split the two. More decisively, the assigned frozen data report no lower-middle-market firms, so no current firm opportunity is available under those inputs.
narrative.demand_durability: The 2022 national survey reports 14.4 million hunters and $45.2 billion of hunting expenditures, supporting a durable activity base. FWS recruitment and retention concerns and the survey-method break argue for roughly flat rather than confidently growing five-year demand, with private-access scarcity as an upside case.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of firm-level preserve data, a zero frozen firm count, code heterogeneity, indirect task and adoption proxies, unknown transaction incidence, regulation and liability, wildlife health, weather, rural connectivity, and discretionary customer spending.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can automate a material share of wildlife-monitoring and administrative work in labor-intensive commercial preserves.
narrative.principal_weakness: The frozen lower-middle-market firm count is zero, and the narrow preserve lens excludes much of the visible hunting ecosystem assigned to other industries or to the product-harvesting portion of this code.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 11421: Hunting and Trapping
sources.supports: Defines the industry as commercial hunting and trapping plus commercial game and hunting preserves.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Provides cross-references placing hunting guides, pest control, accommodation camps, and nature preserves outside NAICS 114210.

sources.title: Automated methods for processing camera trap video data for distance sampling
sources.supports: Reports machine-learning wildlife classification performance and substantial analyst-hour savings in camera-trap processing.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence in the USGS Ecosystems Mission Area: Fish and Wildlife Applications
sources.supports: Describes AI use for species presence, abundance, habitat use, disease detection, population monitoring, and behavior or movement analysis.

sources.title: 2022 National Survey of Fishing, Hunting, and Wildlife-Associated Recreation
sources.supports: Reports 14.4 million hunters and $45.2 billion in hunting expenditures in 2022, including trip, equipment, and other spending.

sources.title: Report Offers Snapshot of Hunters and Anglers in the U.S.
sources.supports: Notes 14.4 million hunters in 2022 and indications of longer-run hunting participation decline that motivate recruitment, retention, and reactivation.

sources.title: Fishing and Hunting Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Describes physical hunting and trapping tasks, regulation and licensing, self-employment, and projected occupational demand.
